Founder.title: I ran dispatch before I automated it.
Founder.p1: Seventeen years managing dispatch at Waste Management Los Angeles — including recycLA, a billion-dollar franchise.
Founder.p2: I know what a missed call costs, and what a dropped route does to a week.
Founder.p3: Now I build the tools I wished I had: agents and platforms that keep the operation moving after everyone goes home.

MissedCall.elenaLine: Elena costs less than one missed job.

errors.missedCalls: Enter your missed calls per week

q1.question: What exactly is Elena?
q1.answer: A voice AI agent that answers the phone like a trained intake specialist. She picks up in seconds, qualifies the caller, books the appointment, and logs everything. She's live right now — call (669) 669-4486.

q2.question: Will this work for my industry?
q2.answer: Elena runs personal-injury intake for law firms today. The same agent pattern handles HVAC, plumbing, turf, dental, and junk removal. If your phone rings after hours and jobs walk away, an agent fits.

q3.question: What happens to the info she takes?
q3.answer: It's logged the moment the call ends — name, number, what they need, and when. You get it in your CRM or inbox, and we follow up the same day. No voicemail transcription guesswork.

q4.question: Does this replace my dispatcher or front desk?
q4.answer: No. Agents take the calls nobody was going to answer: after hours, weekends, overflow when the line is busy. Your team keeps the judgment calls.

q5.question: How fast can we launch?
q5.answer: A voice agent trained on your business typically goes live in one to two weeks. Dispatch and operations platforms get scoped on a call — we give you a straight timeline before any money moves.

q6.question: Is the Spanish actually native?
q6.answer: Yes. Written by people, in Spanish, from the start — not machine-translated. Agents switch languages mid-call the same way your customers do.

q7.question: What does it cost?
q7.answer: Less than the jobs you're losing. Run the missed-call calculator with your own numbers, then check the pricing page. In most trades a voice agent costs less per month than one average missed job.

q8.question: Can you plug into my existing tools?
q8.answer: That's the point. Thumbtack and Housecall Pro webhooks, CRMs, calendars, quoting. We build around what you run today — you don't migrate your business to fit the agent.

sam.quote: Started with SEO. Stayed for the chatbot and social. They're the ops team I don't have to hire.

MayaDemo.p2: Elena handles law firm intake. Maya handles home services. Same platform, tailored to your business — English y español.

Rationale.starterFast: You need a focused site live in the next few weeks. Starter fits the scope; Business is there if you add more later.
Rationale.starterChatbotFast: A chatbot for your current site, shipped in weeks. Starter keeps the scope tight.
Rationale.businessBilingualFast: Bilingual done right — authored in both languages — pushes you past Starter. Business covers it without Professional's overhead.
Rationale.businessBoth: A website and a chatbot in one scope. Business handles both without stepping up to Professional.
Rationale.businessMedium: You've got a month or two, which means room to do both well. Business is the right center point.
Rationale.professionalComplex: Both a site and a chatbot, bilingual, on a longer timeline. Professional gives you the room and a dedicated PM.
Rationale.professionalFlexible: No rush, and you want it done right in both languages. Professional covers the depth without cutting corners.

Hero.videoLabel: Abstract animation of a lead pipeline in motion
Hero.pitchStart: A lead that comes in at 11:30pm gets
Hero.pitchEm: answered, priced, and handed a bookable time slot in under a minute
Hero.notePre: A plain-English walkthrough of an AI-run operation AbeMedia built for a real Phoenix moving & junk-removal company. Everything marked
Hero.notePost: is running in production right now.

captions.rest: — no humans needed for the happy path
→ instant friendly reply + a human is emailed
flows back to the lead platform automatically

Stage1.title: A lead arrives
Stage1.lede: The moment a customer submits a request on the lead platform, it lands in the system instantly — a webhook: their computer calls ours the second it happens. Nobody checks, refreshes, or watches an inbox.

log.text: lead stored permanently — before anything else runs

safety: Every lead is stored permanently before anything else happens — nothing can be lost.
The connection is authenticated; requests from anyone but the lead platform are rejected.
A lead the system doesn't recognize is stored and a human is emailed — never silently dropped.

Stage2.title: The brain decides
Stage2.lede: The stored lead immediately runs through a decision pipeline. First question: what kind of job is this?

rows.text: A full instant quote from the company's own pricebook — priced, checked, and sent in seconds.
An instant rate card matched to the size of the job — including spotting when a truck isn't actually needed.
Specialty work gets its own rate card, sent just as fast.
A friendly “let me get you an exact price” reply goes out instantly — a human sets the price.

Stage2.ruleEm: AI reads the message. The pricebook sets the price.
Stage2.ruleBody: The AI only identifies what the customer has — “a couch and a mattress in the garage” matches rows in the company's own pricebook, the same prices a human would quote. Hard price floors can only ever raise a price, never lower it. And an unusually large job is never auto-quoted — it goes to a human.

flow.text: “a couch and a mattress in the garage”

Stage3.lede: About a minute after the lead arrives, the customer is holding a real answer: a friendly, human-sounding message with the price or rate — and genuine open time slots pulled from the company's live calendar.
Stage3.casesIntro: Whatever the customer asked about scheduling, the reply meets them there:

cases.text: Tuesday at 9am works on our end — you can lock it in right here:
cases.meta: slot verified open at send time ✓

cases.h: They asked for a time that's taken
cases.text: Tuesday 6pm isn't open on our end, but we do have Tuesday 3–5pm or Wednesday 9–11am — grab either one here:

cases.h: They didn't name a time at all
cases.sub: Lead with the soonest real openings.
cases.text: We have openings as soon as tomorrow morning — pick whatever works best right here:

Stage3.verifyNote: Every slot offered is verified open at that moment — the system never guesses. If the calendar is ever slow or unreachable, the message quietly falls back to standard wording; the customer never sees an error.
Stage3.gatesTitle: Quality gates every message must pass before sending

gates.rest: — short and warm, never an interrogation
— never offers a slot the crew can't realistically make

gates.b: Any failed check goes to a human

Stage4.title: Humans stay in the loop
Stage4.lede: The system knows what it doesn't know. Anything unusual is escalated — and speed is never sacrificed: the customer still gets an instant friendly reply while an alert email heads to the owner's inbox within minutes.

flags.rest: The customer asked for a time the crew doesn't normally run — a human decides case by case.
This thread is waiting on you.
A paid lead looks refundable — see step 8.

emailRows.v: Requested an evening slot — outside working hours
Instant rate card + friendly holding line

Stage4.emailNote: Every escalation email carries the customer's details, the reason, and exactly what was already sent — so the owner replies informed, not cold.

Stage5.lede: Most customers don't answer the first message — and most companies never follow up. This one does, automatically, spaced out over the following days.
Stage5.stopRule: The instant a customer replies, all nudges stop.
checks: Nudges only send during business hours — never at night, enforced by a hard overnight blackout.
A separate watchdog makes sure no escalated lead sits unanswered during business hours.

Stage6.title: The customer books
Stage6.lede: Every reply carries a live online booking link. The customer picks their own slot from real availability — no phone tag.

steps.b: The customer taps the link and picks a slot
steps.s: Real openings only — same-day is possible when the calendar allows it.

steps.b: The calendar checks crew schedules and books the job
steps.s: Assigned straight to an available crew member — no dispatcher needed.

steps.b: The CRM creates the customer and job records
steps.s: The system deliberately never duplicates what the CRM already does best.

Stage6.enrichBody: Customers often type their real phone number or a gate code into the chat. The system spots these and prepares updates for the customer record.
strip: emailed as a digest for review
Stage6.enrichNote: Currently emailed for review rather than written automatically — nothing touches the records without human eyes on it.

Stage7.lede: The calendar reports every job event — scheduled, on the way, started, completed, canceled. The status-sync loop maps each one back to the original conversation and posts the update automatically.

sync.dst: “job complete” — which feeds the platform's review prompts
recorded correctly on the platform side

checks.rest: when the platform is briefly unavailable.
Posting stayed off until real job events had been observed end-to-end — then it was flipped on.

Stage7.kill: One kill switch turns posting off instantly.

Stage8.title: Clean books
Stage8.lede: Running quietly in the background, protecting the lead budget.

panels.title: Weekly budget watch
panels.body: Email alerts as the weekly lead budget fills up — no surprise spend.

panels.title: Refund watchdog · found money
panels.body: Lead platforms charge for leads even when the customer ghosts. The system scans paid leads where the company replied and the customer never engaged, and emails a refund recommendation before the filing deadline.

panels.title: Full audit trail
panels.body: Every lead, every message, every decision, and every price is stored with timestamps — nothing is a black box.

rules.b: AI extracts facts; the pricebook sets prices.
rules.s: Never the reverse. The AI cannot invent a dollar amount.

rules.b: Every risky feature has a kill switch.
rules.s: One setting flips it off instantly — no redeploy, no downtime.

rules.b: When in doubt, escalate to a human.
rules.s: With the customer already holding a friendly instant reply, so no speed is lost.
A time slot is only offered if it's genuinely open in the calendar right now.

rules.b: If a service fails, the experience degrades to “normal” — never to “broken.”
rules.s: The customer never sees an error.

Board.title: What runs where, today
Board.lede: No promises — this is what's already shipped for this client versus what's queued.

rows.cap: Instant replies: quotes, rate cards, soft-holds
Real-availability slot offers + instant booking link
Escalation emails, follow-up nudges, response watchdog

rows.small: Runs fully — results emailed as a digest for review

rows.cap: Job status posted back to the lead platform
True auto-booking — the system creates the appointment itself
rows.small: Deliberately later: booking stays in the customer's hands for now

Cta.title: Your industry, same machine
Cta.lede: Everything you just scrolled through runs for one Phoenix company today. The same machine — instant answers, real availability, polite persistence, humans on the edge cases, clean books — fits HVAC, plumbing, landscaping, dental, law, and any business where leads arrive faster than people can answer them.

rows.d: One computer calling another the instant something happens — no polling, no refreshing, no inbox-watching.
The company's system of record for customers, jobs, and schedules.
A page where the customer picks a real open slot themselves — no phone tag.
The system handing a decision to a human — with the customer already holding a friendly instant reply.
The company's own price list. The only place a dollar amount can come from.
